model_summary: Initial observation model for postpartum emotional distress focusing on eight operationalized criteria (identity change, bonding, entrapment, guilt, overwhelm, tearfulness, self-criticism, perceived invalidation). Predictors prioritized to be modifiable and measurable at the EMA / daily / passive timescales: sleep (subjective + passive), self-compassion practice, cognitive reappraisal use, perceived social validation, respite hours, physical activity (steps), and social-media/screen exposure. Design emphasizes within-person variance (1–9 Likert where possible), coherent sampling (6 EMA prompts/day for momentary states, 1 daily morning diary for sleep/trait items, passive daily/ per-prompt aggregation), and parsimony to support later gVAR estimation while preserving coverage of BIO/PSYCHO/SOCIAL levers.
variable_selection_notes: Selected criteria preserve full complaint resolution (all 8 original criterion variables retained). Predictor selection prioritized high HyDe/LLM signals and mechanistic plausibility: sleep (top-ranked in LLM mappings), self-compassion/emotion-regulation (HyDe top-ranked), cognitive reappraisal/behavioral activation (CBT signals), social support/respite (SOCIAL signals), activity and passive digital exposures as contextual modifiers. Trade-offs: limited number of overlapping constructs to reduce collinearity (chose one broad self-compassion measure and one cognitive-reappraisal measure rather than multiple overlapping self-regulation items). Passive signals chosen to increase objective variance but are optional if device access is lacking. Uncertainty/bias notes: identity-change (C01) is more trait-like (low within-person variability) and will likely require detrending or differencing; perceived bonding (C02) shows ceiling risk; screen-time effects are potentially nonlinear, so we model them as such rather than forcing monotonic signs.

momentary_ema.notes: Fixed 6 blocks with small random jitter to reduce predictability and memory biases. Use single-screen short EMA (<30s) per prompt to minimize burden.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary (07:30) capturing last-night sleep subjective rating and previous day's respite hours / summary of major events. Use brief structured items.

passive_streams.aggregation_strategy: passive_continuous then aggregate per-prompt and daily
passive_streams.notes: Collect continuous phone/wearable-derived steps and screen-time and sleep-tracking where available. Aggregate steps and screen-time into 'since last prompt' windows for per-prompt alignment; sleep minutes aggregated nightly for morning diary.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Expect ~168 momentary + 28 daily observations; after preprocessing (imputation, detrending, z-scoring), gVAR with this individual time series is feasible. Control for nonstationarity (C01 low within-person variance) by detrending or treating C01 as slow-moving exogenous or using difference series. Use person-mean centering for predictors and criteria before VAR modeling.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 Likert scales for subjective constructs to increase variance. Passive step/screen aggregates will add continuous variability. Encourage wearing phone/wearable and enable OS sleep tracking to support objective sleep minutes.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: EMA burden (6/day) may induce missingness; implement brief prompts, user reminders, and allow Grace windows. Passive streams mitigate missingness but depend on permissions and device availability; collect meta-data on missingness and device status.

safety_notes: No immediate acute self-harm or psychosis signals in the complaint text. Monitor responses for emergent safety risk (explicit suicidal ideation, intent, or severe functioning loss); if reported, encourage immediate contact with local emergency services or a clinician. For questions about resource scarcity (childcare), provide referral options. Passive data collection requires explicit consent and clear privacy explanations.

criteria_variables.label: Altered sense of self since childbirth

measurement.notes: Trait-like; expect slow changes across weeks. Recommended to model as slow-moving or difference series in gVAR.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering and detrend (first-difference or low-pass filter); consider modeling as exogenous slow variable

predictor_variables.label: Subjective sleep quality (last night)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Poor sleep quality increases emotional reactivity, low coping capacity, and tearfulness; improving sleep can rapidly reduce negative affect.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Establish consistent sleep window, optimize sleep environment (dark, cool), brief sleep hygiene routines; discuss with clinician if persistent.

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how well did you sleep overall? (1 = very poorly, 9 = very well)
measurement.notes: Morning diary item aligned with passive sleep minutes; subjectivity captures restfulness.

expected_effects.rationale: Improved sleep over nights gradually supports restored sense of self; lag may be days.
Feeling rested may modestly strengthen positive affect toward baby.
Poor sleep increases perceived entrapment via reduced coping capacity.
Better sleep reduces negative affect and guilt intensity.
Sleep has strong immediate effects on subjective overwhelm and coping.
Sleep improves emotional reactivity and reduces tearfulness the next day.
Sustained better sleep reduces negative self-evaluation over days.
Indirect effect: better sleep increases capacity to tolerate invalidation.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Sleep items are low risk; if severe insomnia suspected, recommend clinical follow-up.

feasibility.notes: Morning diary is low burden; high compliance expected.

The measurement item 'Last night, how well did you sleep overall? (1 = very poorly, 9 = very well)' aligns exactly with the variable's label and scale. The leaf resides under BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / self_assessment_and_logs / daily_and_retrospective_logs, which fits the described daily, within-person, EMA-based sleep predictor design. Mechanism rationale: poor sleep quality increases emotional reactivity and reduces coping resources, making this item a mechanistically plausible, state-sensitive predictor of emotional distress components. Pseudoprofile context supports using a daily sleep quality rating as a modifiable, momentary signal within gVAR modeling.

predictor_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / activity_fitness_and_biomechanics / activity_and_energy / step_counting_wearable
predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: The variable describes physical activity as steps counted by a wearable/phone; the closest leaf is 'step_counting_wearable' under BIO / movement_physical_function_and_somatic_care / physical_monitoring_and_assessment / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / activity_fitness_and_biomechanics / activity_and_energy / *step_counting_wearable' which explicitly captures device-derived step counts. It matches: measurement.channel: phone/wearable step counts; measurement.item_or_signal: Steps recorded in the window since last prompt; measurement_sampling_per_day: 6; per-prompt aggregation into windows. This is the most specific operational construct for steps on a per-prompt window, within the BIO domain, aligning with the pseudoprofile focusing on physical activity as a modifiable EMA context.
predictor_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Physical activity (steps per prompt-window) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Light activity influences mood and fatigue and can reduce tearfulness and overwhelm over short timescales. | Intervention examples: Short walks, paced activity during baby naps. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Steps recorded in the window since last prompt (device-derived) | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Movement_Physical_Function_and_Somatic_Care > Aerobic_and_Cardiorespiratory_Movement

predictor_variables.label: Screen time / social media minutes (since last prompt)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Digital exposure/social media may have nonlinear effects on mood — short social connection vs. longer passive browsing increasing guilt/comparison.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Time-limited social-media use, curated content, brief social-media breaks.

measurement.channel: phone screen-time API (minutes per app or aggregated)
measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes of screen time (or social-media app usage) since last prompt
measurement.notes: Expect nonlinear associations; measure continuously and test for U-shaped patterns.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p; also consider quadratic terms or splines in modeling

expected_effects.rationale: Short social contact may reduce tearfulness, while prolonged passive browsing may increase negative comparisons and tearfulness.

predictor_variables.risk_ethics_notes: Privacy-sensitive; obtain explicit consent and explain aggregation; do not collect content.

feasibility.notes: Depends on OS permissions and app-level aggregation availability.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf for Screen time / social media minutes. Variable label matches exactly; the leaf 'screen_time_selfreport' encodes minutes of screen time exposure from digital devices, aligning with the DIGITAL_SOCIAL_ENVIRONMENT/SCREEN_TIME concept within the SOCIAL domain. It represents the same construct (digital social exposure) and is the most specific leaf among candidates for this predictor. Although the measurement mode described is passive_phone_sensor, and the leaf denotes self-reported screen time, the underlying construct (minutes of screen time) is the same, making this the closest match to the intended predictor. This fits the pseudoprofile emphasis on social-media/screen exposure as a modifiable, EMA-friendly lever in within-person analyses.
predictor_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Screen time / social media minutes (since last prompt) | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Digital exposure/social media may have nonlinear effects on mood — short social connection vs. longer passive browsing increasing guilt/comparison. | Intervention examples: Time-limited social-media use, curated content, brief social-media breaks. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Minutes of screen time (or social-media app usage) since last prompt | Prior ontology hint: [SOCIAL] > Digital_Social_Environment > Digital_Social_Environment_Solutions

edges.notes: Better subjective sleep last night expected to reduce next-day overwhelm.
Self-compassion practice expected to reduce guilt quickly.
Additional respite hours directly lower entrapment and overwhelm.
Activity bursts may reduce tearfulness within hours.
Screen time may have U-shaped effects on tearfulness (short contact vs prolonged passive browsing).

expected_collinearity_risks: P03 (self-compassion) vs P04 (reappraisal): moderate collinearity of adaptive emotion-regulation skill use.
P01 (subjective sleep quality) vs P02 (objective sleep minutes): high collinearity expected; choose single or orthogonalize if necessary.
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: C02 (positive bond) may show ceiling effects (responses clustered toward high values); consider modeling limited variance.
P06 (respite hours) may be zero-inflated leading to floor effects on many days.
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1-9 Likert anchored scales (already applied) to increase within-person variance.
Encourage passive data capture to add continuous variance (steps, screen-time).
If C01 shows too little variance, treat as slow-moving covariate or collect weekly identity-reflection items.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: Pharmacotherapy and invasive neuromodulation (e.g., TMS) deprioritized for initial within-person gVAR exploration due to complexity and need for clinical oversight.
Highly overlapping self-regulation subscales were avoided to reduce collinearity (kept one self-compassion and one cognitive reappraisal measure).
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 28 days, 6 EMA prompts/day (168 momentary points) + 28 daily diaries and passive streams, there is sufficient temporal resolution for single-subject gVAR with 8 criterion and 8 predictor nodes after preprocessing. Recommend person-mean centering, detrending for slow-moving variables, and handling missingness with interpolation or multiple imputation tailored to time-series.

criteria.label: Altered sense of self since childbirth
criteria.query_text_used: Altered sense of self since childbirth | Operational item/signal: Thinking about the period since having your baby, how much do you feel like 'yourself' compared to before the baby? (1 = completely like myself, 9 = not at all like myself) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Trait-like; expect slow changes across weeks. Recommended to model as slow-moving or difference series in gVAR.

mapping.rationale: The variable 'Altered sense of self since childbirth' corresponds to identity change in the postpartum period. The EMA item asks how much you feel like yourself since childbirth, which directly targets self_concept. Among candidates, idx 1144 maps to NON-CLINICAL / AGE-SPECIFIC / unspecified_stage / emotional_development / identity_development / self_concept, providing the most specific fit for an identity/self-concept construct in a postpartum context. The measurement mode (EMA_self_report) and morning diary timing align with the variable's daily/within-person assessment goals. Conceptual alignment is reinforced by the trait-like note (slow changes) that can be modeled as a slow-moving or differenced series within gVAR, consistent with a self-concept trajectory.

source_variable.label: Altered sense of self since childbirth

measurement.item_or_signal: Thinking about the period since having your baby, how much do you feel like 'yourself' compared to before the baby? (1 = completely like myself, 9 = not at all like myself)
measurement.notes: Trait-like; expect slow changes across weeks. Recommended to model as slow-moving or difference series in gVAR.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering and detrend (first-difference or low-pass filter); consider modeling as exogenous slow variable

criteria.label: Positive emotional bond with the baby
criteria.query_text_used: Positive emotional bond with the baby | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how strongly did you feel affectionate/connected to your baby? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Ceiling risk possible because 'love' is present; measure intensity to preserve variance.

mapping.rationale: Best match is the leaf node 'emotional bonding' within 'parent-child relationships'. The variable label is 'Positive emotional bond with the baby', and the measurement item asks about the strength of affectionate/connected feelings toward the baby since the last prompt, on a 1–9 scale via EMA. This leaf precisely captures the intended construct (emotional bonding in the infant–caregiver dyad) at a postpartum context, aligning with the measurement mode and recall window. It is more specific than broader 'social bonding' or 'affection for others' leaves, and most directly maps to the target criterion.

criteria.label: Perceived entrapment / loss of autonomy
criteria.query_text_used: Perceived entrapment / loss of autonomy | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel trapped or limited by your caregiving role? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Momentary fluctuations expected (respite, help, naps may alter).

mapping.rationale: Variable maps to perceived entrapment / loss of autonomy. The leaf (NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / psychological / autonomy / boundary setting / establishing limits / defining personal limits) captures autonomy-related boundary management as the mechanism for entrapment, aligning with the item about feeling trapped by caregiving role. It is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this autonomy-focused state in EMA context.

measurement.notes: Momentary fluctuations expected (respite, help, naps may alter).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: person-mean centering; consider logit transform if bounded clustering

criteria.label: Excessive guilt about feelings/parenting
criteria.query_text_used: Excessive guilt about feelings/parenting | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how guilty did you feel about your feelings or parenting? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Direct target of self-compassion and cognitive reappraisal interventions.

mapping.rationale: Best match is the leaf 'peripartum (antenatal or postpartum) depression / excessive_guilt_or_worthlessness' (idx 29676). The variable label is Excessive guilt about feelings/parenting, captured via EMA self-report with a 1–9 scale, since-last-prompt assessment. This leaf directly represents excessive guilt (and worthlessness) in a postpartum context, aligning with the postpartum distress focus and the target of guilt-related mechanisms. It is more specific to the postpartum timeframe than general guilt leaves and maps to the intended mechanism (modifiable via self-compassion and cognitive reappraisal).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how guilty did you feel about your feelings or parenting? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely)
measurement.notes: Direct target of self-compassion and cognitive reappraisal interventions.

criteria.label: Subjective overwhelm / low coping capacity
criteria.query_text_used: Subjective overwhelm / low coping capacity | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how overwhelmed or unable to cope did you feel? (1 = not at all, 9 = completely overwhelmed) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: High expected within-person variance; sensitive to sleep, respite, and coping skills.

mapping.rationale: C05 (Subjective overwhelm / low coping capacity) is an EMA self-report of momentary distress. The closest operational leaf is idx 5402: 'feeling exhausted' (emotional / subjective affective feeling). Exhaustion directly reflects reduced coping resources and is likely to scale with overwhelm on a 1–9 Likert in six prompts per day. This leaf aligns with measurement mode (EMA_self_report), item phrasing (since_last_prompt: 'how overwhelmed or unable to cope'), and the within-person variance emphasis in the pseudoprofile (sleep respite and coping skills modulate exhaustion/overwhelm). It is the most specific within the candidate set that captures the intended construct without overfitting to unrelated states.

measurement.notes: High expected within-person variance; sensitive to sleep, respite, and coping skills.

criteria.label: Low frustration tolerance / frequent tearfulness
criteria.query_text_used: Low frustration tolerance / frequent tearfulness | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how tearful or emotionally reactive were you to small triggers? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Responsive to sleep, self-regulation skills, and short-term supports.

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 'Low frustration tolerance / frequent tearfulness' is an EMA self-report of tearfulness and emotional reactivity to triggers. The most specific CRITERION leaf that matches tearfulness/emotional lability in this postpartum-focused context is 29670 'tearfulness_or_emotional_lability' (peripartum depressive disorders). This leaf directly encodes tearfulness and emotional lability, aligning with the measurement item (tearful or emotionally reactive to small triggers, 1–9). The postpartum context from the pseudoprofile supports the relevance, without overfitting to it.

criteria.label: Self-criticism after emotional episodes
criteria.query_text_used: Self-criticism after emotional episodes | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, after feeling upset, how much did you judge yourself negatively (e.g., 'bad parent')? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Direct target for compassion-focused and cognitive reappraisal interventions.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Self-criticism after emotional episodes' maps most directly to a state-level self-criticism construct. The EMA item asks about negative self-judgment after feeling upset (since last prompt), which aligns with 'self-critical rumination' as a cognitive response to distress. Among candidates, idx 19031 ('self_critical_rumination') is the closest operational leaf, providing the most specific match for momentary self-criticism and enabling mechanistic targeting (e.g., compassion-focused or cognitive reappraisal interventions) within the pseudoprofile context.

measurement.notes: Direct target for compassion-focused and cognitive reappraisal interventions.

criteria.label: Perceived minimization / lack of validation from others
criteria.query_text_used: Perceived minimization / lack of validation from others | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel that others dismissed or minimized how you feel? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Important social-context variable; may moderate responsiveness to interventions.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Perceived minimization / lack of validation from others' maps to the 'emotional validation' construct. The EMA item asks whether others dismissed or minimized how you feel, which directly corresponds to whether your emotions are emotionally validated by others. Among candidates, idx 3078 is the most specific operational leaf capturing emotional validation in a social/validation context, aligning with the measurement item, channel (EMA self-report), and within-person fluctuation aims. This leaf also coheres with the broader SOC/validation domain in the pseudoprofile context without overfitting trait-level assumptions.

source_variable.label: Perceived minimization / lack of validation from others

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, to what extent did you feel that others dismissed or minimized how you feel? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely)
measurement.notes: Important social-context variable; may moderate responsiveness to interventions.

predictors.label: Subjective sleep quality (last night)
predictors.query_text_used: Subjective sleep quality (last night) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Poor sleep quality increases emotional reactivity, low coping capacity, and tearfulness; improving sleep can rapidly reduce negative affect. | Intervention examples: Establish consistent sleep window, optimize sleep environment (dark, cool), brief sleep hygiene routines; discuss with clinician if persistent. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Last night, how well did you sleep overall? (1 = very poorly, 9 = very well) | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration > Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Best match is idx 4548: the label sleep_quality_rating directly corresponds to 'Subjective sleep quality (last night)' with a 1–9 Likert scale in a morning diary (EMA_self_report). The measurement item 'Last night, how well did you sleep overall? (1 = very poorly, 9 = very well)' aligns exactly with the variable's label and scale. The leaf resides under BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / self_assessment_and_logs / daily_and_retrospective_logs, which fits the described daily, within-person, EMA-based sleep predictor design. Mechanism rationale: poor sleep quality increases emotional reactivity and reduces coping resources, making this item a mechanistically plausible, state-sensitive predictor of emotional distress components. Pseudoprofile context supports using a daily sleep quality rating as a modifiable, momentary signal within gVAR modeling.

source_variable.label: Subjective sleep quality (last night)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Poor sleep quality increases emotional reactivity, low coping capacity, and tearfulness; improving sleep can rapidly reduce negative affect.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Establish consistent sleep window, optimize sleep environment (dark, cool), brief sleep hygiene routines; discuss with clinician if persistent.

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night, how well did you sleep overall? (1 = very poorly, 9 = very well)
measurement.notes: Morning diary item aligned with passive sleep minutes; subjectivity captures restfulness.

expected_effects.rationale: Improved sleep over nights gradually supports restored sense of self; lag may be days.
Feeling rested may modestly strengthen positive affect toward baby.
Poor sleep increases perceived entrapment via reduced coping capacity.
Better sleep reduces negative affect and guilt intensity.
Sleep has strong immediate effects on subjective overwhelm and coping.
Sleep improves emotional reactivity and reduces tearfulness the next day.
Sustained better sleep reduces negative self-evaluation over days.
Indirect effect: better sleep increases capacity to tolerate invalidation.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Sleep items are low risk; if severe insomnia suspected, recommend clinical follow-up.

feasibility.notes: Morning diary is low burden; high compliance expected.

predictors.label: Objective sleep minutes (night)
predictors.query_text_used: Objective sleep minutes (night) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Total sleep minutes objectively index recovery; shorter sleep is linked to worse mood and coping. | Intervention examples: Align sleep schedule; obtain wearable/phone-based sleep tracking; discuss clinical options if severely short. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Total sleep minutes last night (device-estimated) | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration > Sleep_Devices_and_Technologies

mapping.rationale: Variable label: Objective sleep minutes (night); measurement item: Total sleep minutes last night (device-estimated); mechanism: actigraphy-based tracking (wearable/phone sensor) provides objective, passive nightly sleep duration estimates. The leaf ACTIGRAPHY_DEVICE under BIO / Sleep_circadian_and_restoration / Sleep_devices_and_technologies / Tracking_and_feedback / *actigraphy_device matches the device-estimated nightly sleep minutes and passive measurement mode described (passive_phone_sensor, last night, 1 per day). This is more specific and mechanistically aligned than subjective sleep diaries or generic wearables, making it the best leaf for this predictor.

source_variable.label: Objective sleep minutes (night)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Total sleep minutes objectively index recovery; shorter sleep is linked to worse mood and coping.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Align sleep schedule; obtain wearable/phone-based sleep tracking; discuss clinical options if severely short.

measurement.channel: phone/wearable sleep tracking (OS/native app or connected wearable)
measurement.item_or_signal: Total sleep minutes last night (device-estimated)
measurement.notes: Requires user permission for sleep data; fallbacks to self-report if unavailable.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p for extreme tails; person-mean centering

expected_effects.rationale: Shorter sleep linked to greater next-day overwhelm.
Objective sleep loss increases emotional reactivity.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Passive collection requires consent; be transparent about data use.

feasibility.notes: Feasible if user grants permissions and has device; otherwise missingness likely.

predictors.label: Self-compassion practice / self-soothing (use intensity)
predictors.query_text_used: Self-compassion practice / self-soothing (use intensity) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Self-compassion practices directly reduce guilt and self-criticism and improve emotion regulation. | Intervention examples: Brief self-compassion scripts, compassionate phrasing, short guided audio (1-5 minutes). | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, when you were upset, how compassionate were you toward yourself? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Core_Psychological_Skills_Toolkits > Compassionate_Self_Relating_and_Inner_Support

mapping.rationale: The variable is Self-compassion practice / self-soothing (use intensity) measured via EMA. The closest leaf is compassionate_self_talk_practice (3647) within PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / emotion_regulation_and_affective_mastery / difficult_emotion_modules / shame_guilt_anger_jealousy_grief / self_relating_and_repair / validate_and_soothe / compassionate_self_talk_practice. This maps directly to inner self-talk as a self-compassion practice to soothe upset and reduce guilt/self-criticism. The measurement item (“Since the last prompt, when you were upset, how compassionate were you toward yourself?”) aligns with evaluating the intensity of compassionate self-talk in momentary states. Mechanistically, self-compassionate self-talk supports emotion regulation and reduces self-criticism, fitting the intended predictor role. The pseudoprofile emphasizes within-person EMA contexts (6 prompts/day), which matches this EMA-based self-talk probe.

source_variable.label: Self-compassion practice / self-soothing (use intensity)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Self-compassion practices directly reduce guilt and self-criticism and improve emotion regulation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief self-compassion scripts, compassionate phrasing, short guided audio (1-5 minutes).

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, when you were upset, how compassionate were you toward yourself? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely)
measurement.notes: Directly targets guilt and self-criticism mechanisms highlighted in the complaint.

expected_effects.rationale: Self-compassion should rapidly reduce guilt and shame-related reactions.
Direct reduction in self-criticism following compassionate self-talk.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Low risk; ensure practices are brief and grounding.

feasibility.notes: EMA items short and tied to prompts; training examples can be provided.

predictors.label: Cognitive reappraisal / thought reframing frequency
predictors.query_text_used: Cognitive reappraisal / thought reframing frequency | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Reappraisal reduces guilt and self-blame by changing appraisal of events; supports identity updating over time. | Intervention examples: Short reappraisal prompts: 'What evidence supports/against that thought?', cognitive cards; guided reframes. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, how often did you try to reframe or challenge negative thoughts? (1 = not at all, 9 = very often) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] > Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies > Cognitive_Restructuring

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf node: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / restructure_and_install / alternative_thinking / cognitive_reappraisal_practice. It exactly corresponds to the variable Cognitive reappraisal frequency (EMA self-report item: 'how often did you try to reframe or challenge negative thoughts?'), and the mechanism rationale matches: cognitive_reappraisal reduces guilt and self-blame by reinterpreting events, supporting adaptive identity updating. The measurement mode and item align with this leaf's construct, and it is the most specific predictor construct among candidates.

source_variable.label: Cognitive reappraisal / thought reframing frequency
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Reappraisal reduces guilt and self-blame by changing appraisal of events; supports identity updating over time.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short reappraisal prompts: 'What evidence supports/against that thought?', cognitive cards; guided reframes.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how often did you try to reframe or challenge negative thoughts? (1 = not at all, 9 = very often)
measurement.notes: Behavioral/cognitive skill use item.

expected_effects.rationale: Reappraisal reduces immediate self-critical thoughts after emotional episodes.
Reframing reduces guilt intensity over short timescales.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Cognitive strategies are low risk; encourage professional support if intrusive thoughts escalate.

predictors.label: Perceived social support / validation
predictors.query_text_used: Perceived social support / validation | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Perceived validation reduces feelings of invalidation and isolation and can moderate guilt and entrapment. | Intervention examples: Short validation scripts, brief supportive check-ins, scheduling a trusted person to listen. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, how supported or understood did you feel by others? (1 = not at all, 9 = completely supported) | Prior ontology hint: [SOCIAL] > Social_Support_and_Belonging > Support_Network_Development

mapping.rationale: Best matching leaf for 'Perceived social support / validation' is under SOCIAL > social_support_and_belonging > belonging_isolation_and_meaning > safety_and_trust_in_support. It explicitly references safety and trust in the support network, aligning with the variable's focus on perceived validation from others. Measurement mode (EMA self-report) and item (Feeling supported/understood by others since last prompt) align with this leaf's social support context. Mechanistically, perceived validation from a trusted network reduces feelings of isolation and invalidation, consistent with the predictor's role in modulating negative affect and self-critical processes. While other leaves address specific interventions or validation skills, this leaf closest captures the ecological perception of being supported in daily life across a social network, as targeted by the pseudoprofile context.

source_variable.label: Perceived social support / validation
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Perceived validation reduces feelings of invalidation and isolation and can moderate guilt and entrapment.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short validation scripts, brief supportive check-ins, scheduling a trusted person to listen.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how supported or understood did you feel by others? (1 = not at all, 9 = completely supported)
measurement.notes: Captures social-context fluctuations; targets perceived minimization (C08) directly.

expected_effects.rationale: Immediate reductions in perceived invalidation following support/validation.
Perceived support alleviates feelings of entrapment by enabling resource sharing.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Asking about social support may surface relationship issues; provide referral options if distress increases.

feasibility.notes: Simple EMA item; likely high compliance.

predictors.label: Respite / childcare hours (available help)
predictors.query_text_used: Respite / childcare hours (available help) | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Objective access to respite directly relieves overwhelm and perceived entrapment and yields immediate improvements in coping. | Intervention examples: Scheduling short breaks, arranging family/friend or paid childcare, micro-respite planning. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Yesterday, how many hours of childcare/respite (by others) did you receive? (enter number of hours) | Prior ontology hint: [SOCIAL] > Family_Parenting_and_Household_Systems > Caregiving_and_Respite_Solutions

mapping.rationale: The variable maps to a SOCIAL domain predictor under Caregiving_and_Respite_Solutions and reflects respite availability for caregivers (parents). The EMA item asks for hours of childcare/respite received yesterday, so the closest leaf is 'practical_parenting_supports / respite_care_for_parents_access', which best captures direct respite services/support relevant to the predictor. Other leaves focus on access/navigation or planning rather than actual hours of respite received. This is the most specific operational construct matching the variable intent and measurement type.

source_variable.label: Respite / childcare hours (available help)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Objective access to respite directly relieves overwhelm and perceived entrapment and yields immediate improvements in coping.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Scheduling short breaks, arranging family/friend or paid childcare, micro-respite planning.

measurement.item_or_signal: Yesterday, how many hours of childcare/respite (by others) did you receive? (enter number of hours)
measurement.notes: Captured in morning diary as sum for previous day; can be sensitive; anonymize if storing free-text.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p for skewed hours; person-mean centering

expected_effects.rationale: Respite has immediate effect on perceived entrapment and overwhelm.
Respite increases coping capacity quickly.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Asking about childcare access may reveal lack of resources; provide resources/referral options.

feasibility.notes: Daily reporting reduces recall bias vs. momentary.

predictors.label: Physical activity (steps per prompt-window)
predictors.query_text_used: Physical activity (steps per prompt-window) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Light activity influences mood and fatigue and can reduce tearfulness and overwhelm over short timescales. | Intervention examples: Short walks, paced activity during baby naps. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Steps recorded in the window since last prompt (device-derived) | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] > Movement_Physical_Function_and_Somatic_Care > Aerobic_and_Cardiorespiratory_Movement

mapping.rationale: The variable describes physical activity as steps counted by a wearable/phone; the closest leaf is 'step_counting_wearable' under BIO / movement_physical_function_and_somatic_care / physical_monitoring_and_assessment / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / activity_fitness_and_biomechanics / activity_and_energy / *step_counting_wearable' which explicitly captures device-derived step counts. It matches: measurement.channel: phone/wearable step counts; measurement.item_or_signal: Steps recorded in the window since last prompt; measurement_sampling_per_day: 6; per-prompt aggregation into windows. This is the most specific operational construct for steps on a per-prompt window, within the BIO domain, aligning with the pseudoprofile focusing on physical activity as a modifiable EMA context.
mapping.leaf_full_path: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / activity_fitness_and_biomechanics / activity_and_energy / step_counting_wearable

source_variable.label: Physical activity (steps per prompt-window)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Light activity influences mood and fatigue and can reduce tearfulness and overwhelm over short timescales.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Short walks, paced activity during baby naps.

measurement.item_or_signal: Steps recorded in the window since last prompt (device-derived)
measurement.notes: Aggregate into per-prompt windows to align with EMA.

expected_effects.rationale: Short activity bursts can reduce immediate emotional reactivity in some individuals.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Passive step data requires permission; may be missing if phone not carried.

feasibility.notes: Useful objective covariate but optional if devices unavailable.

predictors.label: Screen time / social media minutes (since last prompt)
predictors.query_text_used: Screen time / social media minutes (since last prompt) | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Digital exposure/social media may have nonlinear effects on mood — short social connection vs. longer passive browsing increasing guilt/comparison. | Intervention examples: Time-limited social-media use, curated content, brief social-media breaks. | Measurement: passive_phone_sensor | Minutes of screen time (or social-media app usage) since last prompt | Prior ontology hint: [SOCIAL] > Digital_Social_Environment > Digital_Social_Environment_Solutions

mapping.rationale: Best-matching predictor leaf for Screen time / social media minutes. Variable label matches exactly; the leaf 'screen_time_selfreport' encodes minutes of screen time exposure from digital devices, aligning with the DIGITAL_SOCIAL_ENVIRONMENT/SCREEN_TIME concept within the SOCIAL domain. It represents the same construct (digital social exposure) and is the most specific leaf among candidates for this predictor. Although the measurement mode described is passive_phone_sensor, and the leaf denotes self-reported screen time, the underlying construct (minutes of screen time) is the same, making this the closest match to the intended predictor. This fits the pseudoprofile emphasis on social-media/screen exposure as a modifiable, EMA-friendly lever in within-person analyses.

source_variable.label: Screen time / social media minutes (since last prompt)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Digital exposure/social media may have nonlinear effects on mood — short social connection vs. longer passive browsing increasing guilt/comparison.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Time-limited social-media use, curated content, brief social-media breaks.

measurement.channel: phone screen-time API (minutes per app or aggregated)
measurement.item_or_signal: Minutes of screen time (or social-media app usage) since last prompt
measurement.notes: Expect nonlinear associations; measure continuously and test for U-shaped patterns.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: log1p; also consider quadratic terms or splines in modeling

expected_effects.rationale: Short social contact may reduce tearfulness, while prolonged passive browsing may increase negative comparisons and tearfulness.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Privacy-sensitive; obtain explicit consent and explain aggregation; do not collect content.

feasibility.notes: Depends on OS permissions and app-level aggregation availability.
